coredata.dc:title: Meticulously designed red emission carbon dots as efficient and stable luminescent species for rapid latent fingerprint recognition and light emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Jianbo
Chen, Rui
Wang, Linfan
Xing, Haoming
Hu, Houwen
Yang, Xiangdong
Gu, Chenjie
Tang, Siyuan
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of carbon dots (CDs) emitting long wavelength has attracted more attentions due to their unique advantages in biological and optoelectronic fields, including the wide absorption, long PL lifetime and deep-tissue penetration. Herein, we prepare the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) from the 2,4-diaminodiphenylamine (DDA) and thiourea via a solvothermal reaction, which exhibit reliable red emission at 613 nm and possess a high quantum yield (QY) of 34 %. Meanwhile, the structural analysis and theoretical calculation reveal that the sulfur doping from thiourea increases the nitrogen and oxygen content of CDs, leading to the red emission observed in R-CDs. Subsequently, we design an integrated fingerprints recognition system based on the R-CDs phosphors for the imaging of latent fingerprints (LFPs) and fast LFPs recognition, expanding the application of biometric identification and comparison technology based on fluorescence. Finally, we successfully apply the R-CDs into red and white light-emitting diodes, improving plant growth efficiency. As a result, this work provides a new in-depth insight of red emission CDs and expands the application in latent fingerprint recognition and light-emitting diodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission CDs
Sulfur doping
Fingerprint recognition
Light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of R-CDs fluorescent powder for LFPs application
Fabrication of RLED and WLED for plant growing
Possible mechanism of red emission in R-CDs
Application of LEDs for plant cultivation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of R-CDs (Inset: the HR-TEM image). (b) The size distribution of R-CDs. (c) XRD pattern of R-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (e) N 1 s and (f) S 2p of R-CDs. (g) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of R-CDs (Inset: the images of R-CDs solution under daylight and 365 UV light). (h) Emission spectra of the R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (460–580 nm). (i) Two-dimensional excitation-emission matrix graph map for R-CDs.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CDs-u and R-CDs. (b) TEM image of CDs-u (Inset: the HR-TEM image). (c) The size distribution of CDs-u and R-CDs. (d) The FT-IR spectra of CDs-u and R-CDs. (e) High-resolution N 1 s spectra of CDs-u and R-CDs. (f) High-resolution S 2p spectra of CDs-u and R-CDs. (g) DFT calculation results (molecular structure, LUMO, HOMO and energy gap) of CDs with different numbers of graphite nitrogen and CO groups.

(a) The LFPs imaging route diagram using R-CDs powder. The fluorescence images of the R-CDs powder stained LFPs on (b) filter paper, (c) foil and (d) glass substrates under UV light. (e) The magnified images of (d): (1) core, (2) termination, (3) island, (4) bifurcation, (5) eye and (6) crossover. (f) The binarization process of fingerprint image on paper based on the MATLAB procedure. (g) The fingerprint recognition process based on the MATLAB procedure.

The EL emission spectra of the (a) RLED and (b) WLED based on R-CDs and green phosphors/R-CDs, respectively (Inset: the corresponding images of RLED and WLED). (c) The CIE chromaticity diagram for the fabricated RLED and WLED. (d) Panoramic images of pease growth for 7 days under the condition of sunlight, WLED and RLED. The (e) height, (f) weight and (g) leaf width of pease growth for 7 days under the condition of sunlight, WLED and RLED. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The progress in synthesis and applications (LFPs recognition, red and white LEDs) of the prepared R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Meticulously designed red emission carbon dots as efficient and stable luminescent species for rapid latent fingerprint recognition and light emitting diodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG)
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of carbon dots (CDs) emitting long wavelength has attracted more attentions due to their unique advantages in biological and optoelectronic fields, including the wide absorption, long PL lifetime and deep-tissue penetration. Herein, we prepare the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) from the 2,4-diaminodiphenylamine (DDA) and thiourea via a solvothermal reaction, which exhibit reliable red emission at 613 nm and possess a high quantum yield (QY) of 34 %. Meanwhile, the structural analysis and theoretical calculation reveal that the sulfur doping from thiourea increases the nitrogen and oxygen content of CDs, leading to the red emission observed in R-CDs. Subsequently, we design an integrated fingerprints recognition system based on the R-CDs phosphors for the imaging of latent fingerprints (LFPs) and fast LFPs recognition, expanding the application of biometric identification and comparison technology based on fluorescence. Finally, we successfully apply the R-CDs into red and white light-emitting diodes, improving plant growth efficiency. As a result, this work provides a new in-depth insight of red emission CDs and expands the application in latent fingerprint recognition and light-emitting diodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the uniqueness and lifelong persistence of the fingerprint, the fingerprint analysis is identified as a highly reliable method for personal identification since the 19th century [1,2]. Typically, the majority of fingerprints escape visibility to the naked eye, but the secretion of the finger will remain on the body surface with distinctive ridge patterns and are commonly called latent fingerprints (LFPs) [2–4]. Therefore, researchers have explored various methods to detect and identify LFPs, including fluorescent dye staining, powder dusting, vacuum metal deposition and cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming [3,5,6]. However, these methods generally exhibit drawbacks such as low contrast, low sensitivity, low selectivity and high toxicity, limiting the wide application of LFPs imaging [6–8]. Therefore, it is necessary to discover a novel material with low toxicity, strong contrast and excellent luminous stability for LFPs imaging. Besides, previous reports have mainly focused on the imaging of LFPs, neglecting the analysis and comparison of LFPs [5–8]. With the rapid development of biometric and criminal investigation technology, it is of great significance to develop an integrated and fast identification method due to the inefficiency and subjectivity of the traditional manual analysis/identification [9]. Currently, the recognition system combined with computer technology has attracted more attentions because of its rapidity and accuracy [10,11]. By capturing a high-quality LFPs image from the scene and designing a program to digitize the image and compare it with the database, a fast recognition process can be achieved. As far as we know, there have been no successful reports of integrating LFPs detection with an accompanying identification system. Therefore, developing a new material and establishing an integrated analytical system for imaging and comparing LFPs pose urgent challenges for the development of LFPs applications.

Light-emitting diode (LED), as a promising green light source, is widely applied in indoor lighting, street lighting and plant-growth lighting due to their excellent ambient lighting capability [12,13]. Meanwhile, white LEDs (WLEDs) have been widely used in lighting and displays because of the high efficacy, long lifetime and low energy consumption [14]. Currently, WLEDs can be produced using a blue chip with yellow phosphors (dichromatic type) or an ultraviolet chip with multicolor (blue-green–red) phosphors (trichromatic type) [15]. Despite the wide use of the dichroic type due to its low cost and simple process, it exhibits a low color rendering index (CRI) and cold color temperature (Tc) [15,16]. Therefore, developing trichromatic type WLEDs is necessary because the addition of the red element can improve the CRI and widen the luminescence spectrum of WLEDs. In addition, the red light has been proven effective in promoting the carbohydrate synthesis, flowering and fruiting process of plants, demonstrating excellent practical application value [17]. However, many of the light-emitting materials (such as rare earth metals or semiconductor quantum dots) currently used in LED manufacturing exhibit certain disadvantages, such as expensive price, serious pollution and serious toxicity. Hence, it holds immense significance to discover a novel material characterized by low toxicity, outstanding stability and environmental friendliness for the preparation of red emission LEDs (RLEDs) and WLEDs in the pursuit of further development.

Interest in carbon dots (CDs) has surged in recent years because they possess various advantages, including low-cost sources, outstanding photostability, favourable fluorescence property, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [18–20]. Thus, the CDs are widely applied in fluorescent sensing, bioimaging, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and light-emitting diode [19,20]. For example, Li et al. prepared silicon-doped CDs exhibiting bright blue emission, successfully visualizing latent fingerprints with second-level details [21]. Chen et al. reported F-doped CDs with green emission for high-resolution latent fingerprint identification [22]. Wei et al. synthesized CDs with strong green emissions for use in white light-emitting devices [23]. However, most of the reported CDs exhibit blue or green fluorescence, which severely limit their applications ability due to the weak penetrability, serious damage from excitation light and significant background interference. In contrast to CDs with low-wavelength emission, red-emissive CDs offer lower energy, greater fluorescence imaging capability and more stable luminous life, and hence are preferable for imaging and optoelectronic devices applications [24]. In recent years, researchers have successfully synthesized red/near-infrared CDs from various precursors, such as citric acid, amines, phenols and so on [25–27]. Among them, the employment of amines, particularly aromatic amines, has been extensively reported. However, due to the low quantum yield (QY), lack of effective synthesized method and ambiguous fluorescence mechanism, the efficient long-wavelength emission CDs are very scarce. It is noted that heteroatom doping is considered to be highly efficient in regulating the fluorescence behavior of CDs through changing the electronic environment, introducing new energy levels and providing abundant surface groups [28]. In addition to nitrogen doping which is widely used for improving the quantum yield of CDs, the sulfur doping in carbon frames can also cause a significant impact on the properties of CDs [29,30]. This gives a great impetus to develop CDs with long-wavelength emission, high QY and excellent stability for the applications of LFPs recognition and LEDs.

Herein, nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) are prepared using 2,4-diaminodiphenylamine as the nitrogen source, thiourea as the sulfur source and formamide as the solvent. The CDs exhibit bright red fluorescence at 613 nm with the QY of 34 % (Scheme 1 ). The introduction of S element is considered as the primary factor contributing to the elevation of nitrogen and oxygen content in graphite, consequently inducing the observed red emission in R-CDs. Subsequently, the integrated intelligent system based on R-CDs and computer is developed for LFPs imaging and fast LFPs recognition, which realizes fast biometric identification and comparison based on fluorescence. Finally, we successfully utilize the red solid phosphors to prepare efficient RLEDs and WLEDs for indoor lighting to assist in plant growth, effectively improving the plant growth efficiency. This work expands the methodology of preparing red-emissive CDs and shows promising applications in LFPs recognition and light-emitting diodes.

2,4-diaminodiphenylamine (DDA, ≥98 %), thiourea (≥99 %), urea (≥99.5 %), formamide (≥99 %), ethanol (≥99.5 %), polyethylene glycol (PEG, average Mn 6000), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, with an alcoholysis degree of 98–99 %) and rhodamine B (≥95.0 %) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). H2SO4 (≥95.0) and NaOH (≥97.0 %) were obtained from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). All the chemical reagents were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) from the Millipore Water System (Millipore, Billerica, MA) was used throughout the experiment.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan) was used to measure the morphology of R-CDs. The TEM images were analyzed using the Image J software to obtain the particle size of R-CDs. The X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker ModelD8) was utilized to measure the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of R-CDs. The Raman spectrum was collected by a confocal Raman spectrometer with a 532 nm laser. The chemical constituents and functional groups of R-CDs were identified by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Perkin-Elmer Spectrum RX-II) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi). The Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption was measured using the SHIMADZU UV-2450 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were monitored on a Fluoromax 4CL spectrophotometer. The absolute QYs and PL decay curves were measured using time resolved and steady state fluorescence spectrometer FLS 1000 (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). All simulations were performed at the B3LYP level of functional with the at 6-311G++(d, p) basis set using the Gaussian 09 software. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) orbitals were plotted using the Gaussview package. The luminous efficiencies of LEDs were measured through an integrating sphere spectroradiometer system (SPEC-3000A, Measurefine).

Here, the R-CDs were prepared from the DDA and thiourea via the solvothermal method. Initially, 2 mmol (0.398 g) of DDA, 1 mmol (0.076 g) of thiourea and 20 mL of formamide were mixed to form a homogeneous solution, which was then added to the Teflon-lined autoclave. After the autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 10 h, the original solution was filtered using the 0.22 μm qualitative filter paper to get the crude R-CDs. Subsequently, the solvent was removed through rotary evaporator. Then the R-CDs solution was dialyzed with the dialysis bag (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) for 48 h to obtain pure R-CDs, with ultrapure water being replenished every 12 h. Finally, the powder was prepared by freeze-drying (72 h) and stored at 4 °C for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of R-CDs fluorescent powder for LFPs application
ce:section.ce:para: For the R-CDs fluorescent powder, 0.5 g of PEG was dissolved in ethanol and heated at 100 °C until PEG was completely dissolved. Then, 5 mg of R-CDs powder was mixed with the PEG solutions uniformly and allowed to naturally dry at room temperature. Finally, the R-CDs fluorescent powder was obtained by grinding the block R-CDs/PEG in a mortar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of RLED and WLED for plant growing
ce:section.ce:para: For the fabrication of RLED, 1.5 g of PVA and 10 mg of R-CDs were dissolved in ethanol. Then the mixtures were stirred at 70 °C for 20 min until the R-CDs were evenly dispersed in the solution. Subsequently, the mixtures were coated onto the blue LED chips (450 nm) to acquire RLED. For the WLED, 10 mg of green commercial phosphors and 15 mg of R-CDs powder were added to 3 g of PVA, and constantly stirred to form a homogeneous and viscous mixture. Finally, the mixture was coated onto the center of the blue chip to obtain WLED.

The TEM is used to explore the morphology and size distribution of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the prepared R-CDs are spherical and well-dispersed. Meanwhile, the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of R-CDs is given in the inset of Fig. 1(a), revealing a lattice diameter of about 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite carbon [31]. Through the analysis of more than 100 points in TEM image, the size distribution histogram of R-CDs shows an average diameter of 5.2 ± 1.5 nm within the range of 2–9 nm (Fig. 1(b)). Furthermore, the XRD pattern is measured to observe the structure of R-CDs (Fig. 1(c)). It demonstrates a broad peak at 21.4°, which is attributed to the graphene structure with (002) plane [32]. The Raman spectrum of R-CDs, presented in Figure S1, exhibits two peaks at 1327 cm−1 and 1542 cm−1, corresponding to the sp3 structural defects (D band) and graphited sp2 carbon network (G band), respectively. The measured I D/I G ratio for the R-CDs is 0.81, indicating that the R-CDs have a high degree of graphitization [33].

Subsequently, FT-IR and XPS spectra are explored to investigate the surface functional groups and elementary composition of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1(d), there are four distinct absorption peaks at 3243–3503 cm−1, 2863 cm−1, 1625–1590 cm−1 and 1495 cm−1, which are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, CH, CO/CN and CC [33,34]. Meanwhile, two stretching vibration peaks of CNand COC are observed at 1387 cm−1 and 1022 cm−1, respectively [30]. Besides, a specific peak at 2073 cm−1 is observed in RCDs, which will be ascribed to the −SCN bond from the thiourea [35]. The CS vibration bands at 1202 cm−1 can be observed, indicating the successful doping of S element in RCDs [36]. Regarding the XPS full spectrum of R-CDs (Figure S2(a)), it contains four elements of C, N, O and S, which are centered at 285 eV, 399 eV, 525 eV and 167 eV, respectively. The atomic percentages are measured to be 49.54 %, 31.94 %, 15.85 % and 2.67 %, respectively (Figure S2(b)). In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the C 1 s spectrum (Figure S3(a)) has four different peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.0 eV and 287.7 eV, which are attributed to the CC/CC, CS, CO and CN/CO bands, respectively [33,37]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1(e)) contains three peaks at 398.3 eV, 399.0 eV and 400.1 eV, corresponding to the pyridine N, pyrrolic N and graphitic-like N groups, respectively [34]. For the O 1 s spectrum (Figure S3(b)), it can be fitted into two peaks of CO at 530.6 eV and CO at 532.0 eV [36]. Besides, the high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1(f)) shows two peaks, including the S 2p3/2 at 167.5 eV and S 2p1/2 at 168.7 eV [38]. These results indicate the successful introduction of nitrogen and sulfur in R-CDs and abundant functional groups on surface of R-CDs.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra are explored to demonstrate the optical properties of R-CDs. In Fig. 1(g), there are two typical absorption bands at 203 nm and 260 nm, which are ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC [39]. Another absorption peak at 310 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO/CN [39]. Under the excitation at 540 nm, the optimal emission wavelength of R-CDs is 613 nm. Interestingly, the UV–vis spectrum of R-CDs overlaps well with the excitation spectrum in the range of 480–550 nm, indicating that the surface states favor for the red emission of R-CDs [40]. For the solution of R-CDs, it exhibits transparent state under sunlight and red fluorescence under 365 UV light (inset in Fig. 1(g)). In Fig. 1(h), the R-CDs show obvious excitation-emission independence as the excitation wavelength varies from 460 nm to 580 nm, which may be due to the uniform surface state of R-CDs [41]. Furthermore, the three-dimensional excitation-emission spectrum of R-CDs is exhibited in Fig. 1(i), revealing a maximum emission peak at 613 nm under the excitation at 540 nm. The absolute and relevant QY are calculated as 23 % and 34 %, respectively (the details are expressed in Supporting information). Compared with previous reports, the developed R-CDs display the higher QY and superior emission wavelength (Figure S6 and Table S1). In addition, the fluorescence stabilities of R-CDs under different conditions are shown in Figure S7. After storage at room temperature for 24 days and exposure to ultraviolet radiation for 40 h (Figure S7(a-b)), the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs exhibits relatively small changes. Meanwhile, the R-CDs show good stability under different concentrations of NaCl solution (0–1.0 M) (Figure S7(c)). Therefore, the above results prove that the R-CDs exhibit satisfying photostability, suggesting the favorable long-time practicality of R-CDs in real applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of red emission in R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that the mainstream fluorescence emission mechanisms in CDs are quantum size effects or surface state [42]. Currently, numerous studies have reported phenylamine derivatives as carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. But these CDs usually exhibit green or yellow fluorescence via the hydrothermal/solvothermal reactions [31,39,40]. Thus, thiourea may be the key factor for the preparation of R-CDs in our work. In view of this, the urea (without S) is adopted to replace the thiourea. Under the same synthetic conditions (2 mmol of DDA, 1 mmol of urea and 20 mL of formamide, 200 °C for 10 h), the prepared CDs are named as CDs-u. Compared with the luminescent property of CDs-u (Fig. 2 (a)), the prepared R-CDs display a higher QY and superior emission wavelength, meaning the important role of S in the bright red emission of R-CDs. To further elucidate the higher QY of R-CDs, the plots of PL intensity decay versus time have been measured, which effectively reveals the kinetics of the de-excitation of electrons from an excited state to the ground state occurred via radiative and nonradiative pathways [43]. As shown in Figure S8, the PL lifetime (τ) of CDs-u and R-CDs can be fitted as 5.70 ns and 6.17 ns, respectively. As is well known, nonradiative transitions play a decisive role in the QY of CDs. Using the absolute QY and PL lifetime, the radiation rate constants (kr ) and non-radiation rate constants (knr ) of CDs are calculated according to the following equations:[44]
(1)
k r
= Q Y / τ
(2)
k
nr
=
( 1 - Q Y )
/ τ

The corresponding values of kr and knr are presented in Table S2. Strikingly, the knr of R-CDs (1.24 × 10−1 ns−1) is smaller than that of CDs-u (1.56 × 10−1 ns−1), indicating that nonradiative transitions are suppressed after introducing S into the reaction system, leading to the improvement of QY (more details are expressed in Supporting information).

Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 2(b), the TEM image of CDs-u exhibits that the average diameter of CDs-u is 4.8 nm with a standard deviation of 1.2 nm, which is close to the average size of R-CDs (Fig. 2(c)), indicating the quantum size effect is not the factor in the red emission of R-CDs. Therefore, different surface states are considered as the significant reason for the red emission. We infer that the introduction of sulfur element in thiourea is crucial for the formation of red emission CDs. To elucidate the nature of the sulfur element in R-CDs, the FT-IR and XPS spectra are measured on CDs-u. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2(d)), the CDs-u exhibit weaker vibrations at 1360 cm−1 (CN) and 1130 cm−1 (CC) compared with R-CDs, which means the R-CDs have a higher content of graphitic N. Meanwhile, a weaker stretching vibration of the CH band and a stronger band of the CO/CN are observed, indicating that the R-CDs possess more surface oxidation content. In addition, the XPS analysis of CDs-u is also explored to investigate the content of graphite nitrogen and surface oxidation. The full XPS spectrum and high-resolution C 1 s spectrum are shown in Figure S10. Comparing the high-resolution N 1 s and O 1 s spectra of CDs-u with R-CDs, it can be seen that the content of graphite nitrogen gradually increases from 12.24 % (CDs-u) to 26.83 % (R-CDs) (Fig. 2(e)). Furthermore, the CO band increases (26.15 % to 78.91 %) with the decrease of C-O band (73.85 % to 21.09 %) (Fig. 2(f)), which indicates a higher oxidation degree of the R-CDs. Detailed contents of peaks are summarized in Table S4. From the above results, it can be believed that the introduction of S can increase the degree of graphitic nitrogen and surface oxidation content in CDs, leading to the red emission of R-CDs. Whereafter, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations are employed to further verify the mechanism of red emission in R-CDs [45,46]. We have established the molecular models with different numbers of graphitic N or CO groups to simulate the orbital energy levels and band-gaps of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2(g), the band gaps of CDs are gradually decreased, which indicates that the increase of surface groups (graphitic N/CO) can lead to a narrowing of the bandgap and a red-shift of the fluorescence wavelength. These results are consistent with the experiments.

In view of the outstanding optical properties of R-CDs, the R-CDs/PEG solid fluorescent powder is prepared for applications in LFPs imaging and recognition. All fingerprint samples are taken from adult male volunteer. Firstly, the volunteers press their fingers against the substrate to collect sweat. Whereafter, R-CDs powder is sprinkled on the base, followed by removal of excess powder. Finally, the red fingerprint mark becomes clearly visible under UV light (Fig. 3 (a)). Herein, three substrates are selected to display the fingerprint images, including filter paper, foil and glass (Figure S11). Under UV light, the LFPs successfully exhibit red fluorescence on the substrates, and the feature points of level one (the ridges of fingerprint images) are clearly recognized, such as the arches, loops and whorls (Fig. 3(b-d)). Meanwhile, as important features of LFPs analysis, the feature points of level two are also clearly shown in Fig. 3(e), which are marked as (1) core, (2) termination, (3) island, (4) bifurcation, (5) eye and (6) crossover, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence images of LFPs exhibit favourable fluorescence stability with storage at room temperature for two weeks (Figure S12), indicating that the R-CDs possess excellent potential in LFPs imaging.

To realize the accurate and rapid recognition of LFPs, the first step involves utilizing the MATLAB software on a computer to analyze the fingerprint images. In this MATLAB procedure, minutiae features in fingerprint images are extracted to distinguish between different fingerprints, including both level one and level two feature points. Prior to feature points extraction, the original fingerprint images undergoes a three-step process to convert them into a binary model (Fig. 3(f)) [47,48]. Firstly, the colour image of fingerprint is converted to a gray scale image. Subsequently, pollutants in the image such as fur, dust and grain are removed, and the brightness of image is normalized, which can effectively improve the recognition of fingerprint contours and feature points. Finally, the image is binarized, which will turn different pixels into black (0) and white (1) according to the grayscale values. Analysis of the final binary image reveals that the level two feature points of LFPs (core, island and bifurcation) are successfully observed. To facilitate the contrastive analysis of LFPs, we extract the coordinates of the feature points from the binary image. Then the coordinates can be transformed into “m × 2” values to form matrices. As shown in Eq. (3), Z is the matrix of the sample and X is the matrix of the control [49].
(3)
Z =
z
11
z
12
⋮
z
i 1
⋮
⋮
z
i 2
⋮
z
m 1
z
m 2
,
X =
x
11
x
12
⋮
x
i 1
⋮
⋮
x
i 2
⋮
x
m 1
x
m 2

Meanwhile, the Euclidean Distance Formula is utilized to calculate the similarity between the sample matrix and the control matrix [48,50]. In the following Eq. (4),
(
z
i 1
,
z
i 2
)
and
(
x
i 1
,
x
i 2
)
are the coordinates of the feature points, and
( a , b )
is the coordinate of the core point in the image.
(4)
P =
1
m
∑
i = 1
m
1
z
i 1
- a
2

z

- b
2

i 1
- a
2
+
x
i 2
- b
2
- 1
+ 1
,
( i = 1 ,
2 ,
3 ⋯ ,
m )
Herein, P is defined as the similarity score. The larger is the value of P, the higher is the similarity. When the P > 80 %, the two matrices can be considered identical, indicating that the two fingerprints belong to the same person. As shown in Fig. 3(g), the binarized fingerprint image is input into the MATLAB program. After comparing with other fingerprints in the program, accurate identification can be achieved. The detailed process and data from the MATLAB program are shown in Video S1 and Figure S13. In addition, we also compare the standard fingerprint to other sample fingerprints, the similarity scores of the samples are all below 80 % (Figure S14). Therefore, the integrated intelligent system based on R-CDs and MATLAB procedure successfully realizes the LFPs imaging and fast LFPs recognition, indicating that the meticulously designed LFPs recognition system holds great potential for practical applications of LFPs recognition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of LEDs for plant cultivation
ce:section.ce:para: The highly efficient red fluorescence of R-CDs will significantly improve the performance of LEDs. Based on the above results, the R-CDs are utilized in the preparation of both RLED and WLED. Meanwhile, PVA is employed as polymer matrix for R-CDs to prepare R-CDs/PVA composite, which can effectively avoid the aggregation-caused quenching effect of R-CDs [51]. Then the composite is coated onto the blue-LED chip and cured at 70 °C for 30 min to fabricate LEDs. Fig. 4 (a) shows the electroluminescence (EL) spectrum of RLED, which indicates the maximum luminescence wavelength of 608 nm and a luminous efficiency of 18.3 lm w−1 under an operating current of 20 mA. Meanwhile, the EL spectrum of WLED displayed in Fig. 4(b), it covers all visible light area and exhibits a bright warm white emission. Furthermore, the CRI, CCT and luminous efficiency of WLED are measured at 82, 4782 K and 37.6 lm w−1, proving a high-quality white light display performance. Fig. 4(c) exhibits the Commission International d’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of RLED and WLED, which are located at (0.52, 0.40) and (0.31, 0.30), respectively.

In addition, to evaluate the usefulness of RLED and WLED, the application of indoor plant cultivation is adopted to exhibit the capacity of RLED and WLED in promoting plant growth. Herein, the pease is used as the sample. Firstly, pea seeds are selected and sowed into the soil in plastic pots. Then these green plants are divided into three groups (A-B-C). During the experiment, equal amounts of water and manure are put into the A, B and C pots. For plant lighting, all groups receive sunlight for photosynthesis during the daytime. At night, group A uses the plant light supplementary system to provide sunlight, and group B and group C use the WLED and RLED, respectively. Finally, we record the growth situation of A, B and C groups on days 1, 2, 3, 5 and 7, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the white and red LED can effectively improve the overall photosynthesis reaction of peas, with peas grown under RLED lighting showing higher growth efficiency and more robust branches than those under sunlight and WLED. Meanwhile, we record the plant height, fresh weight and leaf width of the peas to analyze the changes under different lights. As shown in Fig. 4(e–g), the height, weight and leaf width of plants in group C are significantly higher than the groups A and B, and group B is slightly larger than group A. The results imply that the RLED and WLED can effectively promote photosynthesis reactions and improve the growth of plant cotyledons.

In this work, the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped R-CDs are prepared via the solvothermal method using DDA and thiourea as precursors. The as-synthesized R-CDs exhibit red fluorescence with a high QY of 34 %. Due to the S doping, the increase of graphite nitrogen and oxygen is considered as the main reason for the red luminescence. Besides, the R-CDs powder is developed for LFPs imaging. Meanwhile, we establish an integrated LFPs recognition system to realize the LFPs imaging and fast recognition, expanding the application scope of R-CDs in LFPs recognition. Finally, the R-CDs are applied to fabricate red and white emission LEDs. The fabricated LEDs exhibit excellent device performance and good ability to promote plant growth, indicating their potential for solid-state LED applications. In a word, our work provides a new strategy for developing red emission CDs and expands the application prospect of R-CDs in fingerprint recognition and light-emitting diodes technology.

Jianbo Cao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rui Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Linfan Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Haoming Xing: Methodology, Investigation. Houwen Hu: Investigation. Xiangdong Yang: Validation, Supervision, Investigation. Chenjie Gu: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Siyuan Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Da Chen: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.152121.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
